adapters.name: robrain
adapters.description: Top-5 decisions by 5-signal composite score, rejected alternatives rendered as warnings.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth

reply.proposal: Keep the API server on Hono and replace the scattered hand-rolled middleware with a small typed middleware kit under packages/api/src/http/middleware. Define one canonical execution order: request ID and structured logging context first, authentication/session or API-key resolution next, per-plan token-bucket rate limiting after the caller is known, then Zod request validation and route handlers. Use Hono's createMiddleware and typed context variables so downstream handlers consistently access principal, apiKey, plan, requestId, and logger without ad hoc parsing. Split composition by route group, e.g. publicRoutes, sessionRoutes, and apiKeyRoutes, while keeping REST/OpenAPI generation from the existing Zod endpoint schemas. Add middleware-order integration tests so future changes cannot silently reorder auth, rate limiting, and logging.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
  - REJECTED: Jest — ESM support still needs transform workarounds; config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt
  - REJECTED: Vitest — Capable, but node:test covers our needs without another dependency to track

reply.proposal: I would not add Redux Toolkit given the prior team decision rejecting it for our dashboard state size and boilerplate cost. Instead, I propose we clean up global state by formalizing the existing Zustand usage into small typed domain stores for session metadata, filters, toasts, and cross-feature UI state, with selectors and action-only mutation boundaries. We should keep server/cache state out of the global store, keep raw session tokens out of JavaScript entirely, and document which state belongs in Zustand versus component-local state or URL state. I would also add lightweight node:test coverage around store actions/selectors so the behavior stays predictable as features grow.
acknowledged_rejections: Redux Toolkit — previously rejected because boilerplate and indirection outweighed benefits for our small dashboard state surface
MobX — previously rejected because implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit

reply.proposal: I would not set up Prisma in packages/api because we already rejected it after measuring cold-start latency and SQL opacity in the 2026-02 spike. Instead, I recommend we keep postgres.js with hand-written SQL, and add stronger type-safety by standardizing per-query typed row mappers, shared table/domain types, and lightweight integration tests that validate result shapes against Postgres. For new endpoints, we should colocate SQL, input validation, row mapping, and OpenAPI response schemas so query behavior stays explicit while API contracts remain typed. If we want additional compile-time help, we can introduce a SQL type-checking/codegen step that works with raw SQL rather than replacing the driver or hiding query plans.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
  - REJECTED: axios — Interceptor chains obscured a double-retry bug in 2026-01; native fetch plus our wrapper is smaller and easier to audit
  - REJECTED: request — Deprecated upstream since 2020; unpatched vulnerabilities
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

reply.proposal: Implement a small billing-provider client in packages/api on top of Node 18+ native fetch and our existing lightweight retry wrapper pattern. The client should centralize base URL, auth headers, request IDs, JSON serialization/deserialization, per-request timeouts via AbortController, and bounded retries with exponential backoff only for retryable network errors and 5xx/429 responses. It should normalize provider failures into typed application errors that include status code, provider error code/message, retryability, and correlation/request IDs, while redacting secrets from logs. Keep the provider surface as explicit methods such as createCustomer, createSubscription, and getInvoice rather than exposing raw fetch calls throughout the codebase, so call sites remain testable and provider-specific behavior stays isolated.

  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab

  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike

reply.proposal: We should keep the session token in a Secure, httpOnly, SameSite=Lax cookie and not make it readable from JavaScript. The SPA should call the REST API with normal browser cookie handling, using same-origin requests or `fetch(..., { credentials: 'include' })` where cross-subdomain requests require it. The API should authenticate from the cookie server-side, so the SPA never receives or attaches the raw token in an Authorization header. If we need CSRF protection beyond SameSite=Lax for specific unsafe cross-site flows, we can add a separate non-secret CSRF token/header without exposing the session token itself.

  - REJECTED: Jest — ESM support still needs transform workarounds; config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt
  - REJECTED: Vitest — Capable, but node:test covers our needs without another dependency to track

  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage

- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

reply.proposal: I recommend we do not add Jest to packages/api and instead wire request-handler unit tests into the existing built-in node:test approach. We can add a packages/api test script that runs the TypeScript build output with `node --test`, keep assertions on `node:assert/strict`, and structure handler tests around direct invocation with lightweight mocked request/response dependencies. For any HTTP-level coverage, we can use the existing REST/OpenAPI contract as the boundary and test handlers without introducing a transform-heavy test framework. This keeps CI dependency-free for testing and avoids repeating the ESM/config drift issues we already hit.
acknowledged_rejections: Jest — previously rejected because ESM support still needs transform workarounds and config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt
Vitest — previously rejected because node:test covers our needs without another dependency to track

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
  - REJECTED: styled-components — Runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible with React Server Components and added ~12KB to the bundle in the spike
  - REJECTED: CSS Modules — Workable but splits styling across files; team preferred colocated utilities
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

reply.proposal: Keep the marketing pages on the existing Tailwind utility-class approach, but structure them so each marketing component owns its visual treatment directly in its JSX. For repeated patterns like hero sections, cards, CTA bands, and logo grids, create small presentational components under apps/dashboard that encapsulate the Tailwind class strings and expose only semantic props. Extend the shared Tailwind config with any new marketing design tokens, spacing, colors, typography, or breakpoints rather than introducing page-local styling systems. This gives the designer colocated, component-owned styling while preserving React Server Components compatibility and the team’s existing styling decision.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike

  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier

reply.proposal: Implement a shared read-through session cache using a PostgreSQL UNLOGGED table keyed by the hashed session token or session ID, with only the minimal authorization/session fields needed on hot API paths plus an expires_at column. Each API instance should first query this cache; on miss, it loads from the canonical sessions table, validates expiry/revocation, and upserts the cache row with a short TTL bounded by the real session expiry. Logout, session rotation, role/permission changes, and revocation paths should delete or update the cache row in the same transaction as the canonical session change to avoid stale authorization data. Add a narrow covering index on the lookup key and a lightweight periodic cleanup for expired rows, treating the UNLOGGED table as disposable and fully rebuildable after crash or deploy. Validate the rollout with latency traces and hit-rate metrics, keeping the implementation inside Postgres so it works consistently across all API instances without adding another datastore.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab

reply.proposal: I do not recommend enabling auto-generated migrations, because the team has an explicit production-history rejection after generated DDL silently dropped a column in staging. Instead, I would keep numbered, forward-only, hand-written SQL migrations as the only path to schema changes, and add CI checks that compare the declared schema definition against the migrated database to catch drift before merge. To reduce manual effort without trusting generated DDL, we can allow a developer-only advisory diff command that prints suggested SQL, but the committed migration must still be reviewed and edited as literal SQL. The implementation should fail CI when the schema definition, migration history, and a freshly migrated Postgres database disagree.
acknowledged_rejections: auto-generated migrations from the schema definition

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code

reply.proposal: Add sparse fieldsets to the existing REST API using a `fields` query parameter, e.g. `GET /v1/users/123?fields=id,name` or, for compound responses, `fields[users]=id,name&fields[teams]=id,slug`. Document the supported field names per endpoint in the OpenAPI spec, validate unknown or unauthorized fields with a clear 400 response, and ensure authorization is enforced before shaping the response. Implement field projection in the service/query layer where practical so we avoid both response overfetching and unnecessary database column reads, while preserving default full-resource responses for backward compatibility. Keep responses cache-friendly by treating the full query string as part of the cache key and returning normal REST resources with only the requested fields included.
